section.title: Who May Dwell on Your Holy Hill
section.sectionRationale: A short entrance liturgy, likely used as worshipers approached the tabernacle or temple, asking who may abide in God's presence and answering with a character portrait of integrity in speech, dealing with neighbors, and use of money. It functions as a searching mirror for anyone approaching worship, and pairs naturally with Psalm 24's similar entrance question.
section.thesis: Entry into God's presence is pictured through a life of integrity, truthfulness, and neighbor love, a portrait no one keeps perfectly on their own, which drives the worshiper toward the one who alone fulfills it completely.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the opening question, who may dwell with God; and the answering portrait of the one who may, described in speech, dealings, and use of money.

units.label: Who may dwell on Your holy mountain?

verses.text: O LORD, who may abide in Your tent? Who may dwell on Your holy mountain?

He who walks with {{tamim|integrity}} and practices righteousness, 
who speaks the truth from his heart,

who has no slander on his tongue, who does no harm to his neighbor, 
who casts no scorn on his friend,

who despises the vile but honors those who fear the LORD, 
who does not revise a costly oath,

who lends his money without interest and refuses a bribe against the innocent. 
He who does these things will never be {{mot|shaken}}.

tamim.gloss: Integrity, blamelessness, wholeness, completeness of character in which a person's inner life and outward conduct match. It does not mean sinless perfection but a settled, undivided honesty before God and others.

mot.gloss: To slip, totter, be shaken or overthrown. The psalm's closing promise is that the one who lives this way will never be mot, will stand firm and secure, a stability grounded in God rather than in circumstances.

basic.context: The psalm opens with a question every worshiper approaching God's presence must ask, who may actually dwell with You. The answer is not a ritual requirement but a character portrait: a person of integrity who speaks truth, does no harm with tongue or hand, keeps promises even at cost, and handles money honestly. Such a person, the psalm says, will never be shaken.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a liturgy meant to search the conscience of every worshiper before entering God's presence, not a checklist anyone fully passes on their own merit. Read alongside the rest of Scripture, it exposes the gap between the entrance requirement and our actual lives, driving us to the one person who has walked this portrait perfectly, Jesus Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The person who may dwell with God walks in tamim, integrity or wholeness, an undivided honesty between inner life and outward conduct. The promised result is that such a person will never be mot, shaken or toppled, because their stability rests in the God they walk with, not in favorable circumstances.
basic.typology: The full and perfect portrait of tamim, integrity, is fulfilled only in Christ, the one truly blameless man, whose righteousness is now given to all who trust Him so that they too may stand unshaken on God's holy hill.
basic.christ: No one keeps this portrait perfectly on their own, which is exactly why the gospel is good news: Jesus Christ walked in complete integrity, spoke only truth, never wronged a neighbor, and kept every costly promise even to His own death, and now His righteousness, not our performance, is what grants us access to dwell with God.
basic.god: God is holy, dwelling on a mountain that requires a certain kind of life to approach, yet He is also the one who, in Christ, supplies exactly the righteousness this psalm requires so that sinners might be welcomed rather than turned away.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 24:3-4 asks the same entrance question with a similar answer. Hebrews 10:19-22 shows believers now have confident access to God's presence through Christ's blood. Micah 6:8 summarizes similar practical righteousness expected of God's people.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's entrance liturgy shows what true fellowship with God requires and how far short every person falls, setting up the entire story's movement toward Christ, who fulfills this portrait completely and opens the way for all who trust Him to dwell with God forever.

context.literary: A short entrance liturgy in question and answer form, likely sung or recited as worshipers processed toward the tabernacle or temple, closely paralleled by Psalm 24:3-6 and structurally similar to prophetic entrance requirements in Isaiah 33:14-16 and Ezekiel 18:5-9.
context.historical: Reflects the practice of ancient Israelite worship where priests or Levites may have questioned and answered worshipers at the gates, examining not ritual purity alone but ethical character as the mark of one fit to approach God.
context.canonical: Part of a family of psalms and prophetic texts asking who may stand in God's presence, a question the whole Bible answers ultimately not through human achievement but through the righteousness of Christ imputed to believers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To examine worshipers' hearts before they approached God's presence, describing the practical, ethical shape that genuine covenant faithfulness takes in daily life, speech, relationships, and money.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm both describes an ideal worshiper's character and prescribes the ethical marks every worshiper should pursue; it is not a legalistic entrance exam earning access but a portrait of the kind of life that flows from genuine covenant relationship with God.

debates.question: Does this psalm teach that a person earns the right to dwell with God through moral achievement?

views.label: The psalm describes the fruit of covenant faithfulness rather than a means of earning access, since the broader Psalter and Torah ground Israel's relationship with God in grace and covenant, not merit
views.case: Israel's covenant relationship with God was established by grace, through the exodus and the covenant at Sinai, before any law was given; this psalm describes the resulting character of a faithful covenant member, not a pre-condition for salvation.

views.label: The psalm sets a genuinely high ethical bar that functioned as a real, searching test of readiness to worship
views.case: The specificity of the list, no slander, no harm, no changed oaths, no interest or bribes, suggests worshipers were meant to take real self-examination seriously before approaching, not merely affirm covenant status in the abstract.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the psalm searches the conscience seriously; Christian reading of the psalm sees that its full ethical portrait is met only in Christ, whose perfect righteousness is credited to those who trust Him, resolving the tension between grace and genuine holiness.

study.originalLanguages: The worshiper who may dwell with God is marked by tamim, integrity, a whole and undivided honesty of life, and is promised as a result that they will never be mot, shaken or toppled, because their security rests in God rather than in circumstance or performance.

typology.type: The person of complete integrity fit to dwell with God, verses 2-5
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, the only truly tamim, blameless man, whose righteousness now qualifies believers to dwell in God's presence.

typology.type: The promise that such a person will never be shaken, verse 5
typology.fulfillment: The unshakeable security believers now have in Christ, Hebrews 12:28, a kingdom that cannot be shaken.

study.christ: This psalm's portrait of integrity, truthful speech, harmless dealing, costly promise keeping, and honest use of money, describes a life no sinner fully achieves. Jesus Christ alone lived this portrait perfectly, and through union with Him believers are counted as those who walk with integrity before God, granted access to dwell in His presence not by their own performance but by Christ's righteousness credited to them.
study.god: God is holy, dwelling on a mountain that requires real ethical fitness to approach, yet the gospel reveals He is also the one who provides, in Christ, exactly the righteousness this psalm requires, welcoming sinners into His presence through no merit of their own.

crossReferences.note: Asks a nearly identical entrance question, describing clean hands and a pure heart as fitting a worshiper to ascend God's holy hill.
Believers now have confident access into God's presence through the blood of Christ, fulfilling what this entrance liturgy required.
Summarizes the practical righteousness God requires, to act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly, echoing this psalm's ethical portrait.

study.oneStory: This psalm asks who may dwell with a holy God and answers with a portrait of integrity no sinner keeps perfectly, driving the whole story of Scripture toward Christ, the one who lived this portrait completely and now grants all who trust Him unshaken access to dwell forever in God's presence.

quiz.q: What question does Psalm 15 open with?

options.text: Who may abide in God's tent and dwell on His holy mountain.
options.note: This entrance question frames the entire psalm's answer.

options.text: How long will the wicked prosper.
options.note: This is a theme found elsewhere in the Psalter, such as Psalm 73, not the opening question here.

options.text: Why has God forgotten His people.
options.note: This kind of lament question appears in other psalms, but Psalm 15 opens with an entrance question, not a lament.

options.text: What sacrifice pleases God most.
options.note: The psalm's concern is ethical character rather than sacrificial ritual specifically.

quiz.q: According to verses 2-3, what marks the person fit to dwell with God?

options.text: Integrity, righteousness, truthful speech, and no slander or harm toward a neighbor.
options.note: This is the psalm's direct ethical portrait in these verses.

options.text: Wealth and social status among the people.
options.note: The psalm's criteria are ethical character, not wealth or status.

options.text: Ritual purity achieved through repeated sacrifices.
options.note: The psalm focuses on ethical conduct rather than ritual sacrifice specifically.

options.text: Physical strength and military success.
options.note: This is unrelated to the psalm's actual criteria, which concern truthful speech and harmless dealing with others.

quiz.q: What does verse 4 say about keeping oaths?

options.text: The one fit to dwell with God keeps a costly oath and does not revise it once made, even to his own hurt.
options.note: This highlights integrity that holds even when honesty costs something personally.

options.text: Oaths should always be renegotiated if circumstances change.
options.note: The psalm commends the opposite, keeping one's word even when it becomes costly.

options.text: Oaths are unnecessary for the righteous person.
options.note: The verse assumes oaths are made and specifically commends keeping them.

options.text: Only oaths made to God, not to other people, must be kept.
options.note: The psalm's broader context includes honest dealing with neighbors and friends generally, not oaths to God alone.

quiz.q: What does the psalm say about money in verse 5?

options.text: The righteous person lends without interest and refuses a bribe against the innocent.
options.note: This describes honest, non-exploitative use of money toward the vulnerable and innocent.

options.text: Money should never be lent under any circumstances.
options.note: The psalm does not forbid lending; it forbids charging interest that exploits a neighbor in need and forbids bribery.

options.text: Wealth automatically disqualifies a person from God's presence.
options.note: The psalm concerns how money is used ethically, not wealth itself as disqualifying.

options.text: Bribes are acceptable if used to help the innocent.
options.note: This misreads the verse, which explicitly refuses bribery against the innocent under any pretext.

quiz.q: How does this psalm point toward Christ?

options.text: It describes a portrait of integrity no sinner keeps perfectly, fulfilled completely only in Jesus, whose righteousness now grants believers access to dwell with God.
options.note: The psalm's high ethical bar drives the reader to the only one who meets it fully, Christ, and to grace received through Him.

options.text: It teaches that anyone can earn entrance to heaven through good works alone.
options.note: This contradicts the gospel and the broader biblical teaching that righteousness before God comes through faith in Christ.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament's teaching on righteousness.
options.note: The psalm's entrance theme connects clearly to New Testament teaching about access to God through Christ, as in Hebrews 10.

options.text: It predicts a specific future event unrelated to personal righteousness.
options.note: This misses the psalm's clear ethical focus, which points toward the righteousness fulfilled in Christ rather than an unrelated prediction.

questions: This psalm asks who may dwell with God and answers with a life of integrity, honest speech, and harmless dealing with others. Where does your own life fall short of this portrait, and how does that shortfall point you toward needing Christ's righteousness rather than your own?
The psalm commends someone who keeps a costly oath even to their own hurt. Where might you be tempted to break a promise or commitment because keeping it has become inconvenient or costly?
Verse 3 warns against slander and casting scorn on a friend. How do your words about others, whether in conversation or online, measure up against this standard?

The psalm commends refusing a bribe against the innocent and lending without exploiting a neighbor's need. Where in your own use of money or influence might you need to choose integrity over self-interest?

The psalm promises that the one who lives this way will never be shaken. Where do you need that kind of unshakeable security right now, and how does resting in Christ's righteousness, rather than your own performance, provide it?

translationNotes.note: Integrity renders tamim, meaning wholeness or blamelessness, an undivided honesty between inner life and outward conduct rather than sinless perfection.
Does not revise a costly oath reflects the sense of keeping one's word even when honoring it brings personal loss or hardship.
Shaken renders mot, meaning to slip, totter, or be overthrown; the promise is of settled stability grounded in relationship with God.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 15:1-5. No em or en dashes present in the source text or in this file.
